moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the classical center pawn, seizing space and preparing to develop our pieces with tempo. This move controls d5 and f5, giving us flexibility in how we want to build our position.
Black immediately challenges our center, which is the principled response to 1.e4. By playing on d5, Black prevents us from establishing a strong pawn duo and fights for central control right away.

We develop our knight to its best square while attacking the e4 pawn. This is a key feature of the Zukertort/Tennison setup—we're not committing to a standard opening structure but rather keeping our options flexible while maintaining piece activity.

Black captures our pawn, which appears natural but walks into tactical complications. By removing our center pawn, Black weakens the d5 square and removes the defender of our knight on f3, setting up the tactical point of our next move.

This aggressive jump directly threatens the f7 pawn, one of the most vulnerable squares in Black's position. We're not playing for material gain alone—we're creating immediate threats that force Black into tactical problems and seize the initiative characteristic of gambit play.

Black defends against the f7 threat while developing a piece and gaining time. The knight on f6 also supports e4 and prepares to reroute if needed, but Black's position remains under pressure since our initiative is worth the sacrificed pawn.

We develop our last undeveloped piece while keeping pressure on e4 and adding support to d5 and other central squares. This move also prepares potential tactics involving the queen and maintains our aggressive posture.

Black develops the bishop to an active square while avoiding pins and keeping an eye on the center. The bishop pressures our side but also moves away from defending the kingside, which will become important in our attack.

Our queen moves to e2, a key square that prepares to support the kingside attack and keeps pressure on the a6-f1 diagonal. By placing the queen here, we prepare moves like Qe3 or later attacks while maintaining flexibility in our piece coordination.

Black centralizes the queen aggressively, seemingly coordinating pieces and threatening our position. However, this bold placement sets up Brigg's Trap—Black's queen becomes vulnerable to tactical blows, and the knight on f6 is overworked defending multiple critical squares, allowing us to launch a powerful attack.
